title: Dipping glaze on a bisque bowl
subtitle: Applying an even coat of liquid glaze by submerging a bisque bowl in a glaze bucket
excerpt: Glaze dipping is the fastest way to apply an even coat of liquid glaze to a bisque piece. The bisque bowl is held by the foot ring, submerged in a bucket of stirred glaze, rotated, and lifted out. The glaze layer dries in seconds as the porous bisqueware absorbs the water. This tutorial covers a single-colour dip glaze on a bowl form.
sourceNotes: Glaze dipping technique derived from first principles.

attrs.body: Liquid glaze contains metal oxides; do not ingest, and wash hands after handling. Do not use the finished bowl for food unless the glaze is explicitly rated food-safe. Keep kiln ventilation open during glaze firing. Never open the kiln above 90°C.
attrs.title: Glaze and kiln safety

content.text: Dipping is the standard studio method for glazing because it is faster than brushing and produces a more even coat than pouring. The porous
bisqueware

absorbs water from the glaze in seconds, leaving a consistent layer of glaze pigment on the surface. The method requires enough liquid glaze to submerge the piece; a bowl 140 mm across needs a bucket holding at least 2-3 litres.
Preparing the bisqueware

Wipe the bowl with a damp sponge to remove kiln dust. Any dust or grease on the surface causes the glaze to absorb unevenly. Allow the surface to dry completely before dipping; a wet bisque surface slows absorption and thins the glaze layer.
Waxing the foot ring
Apply
wax resist

to the foot ring with a dedicated brush. Cover the standing surface and the inner and outer sides of the ring. Allow to dry for 20-30 minutes. The wax prevents glaze adhering to the foot ring; unglazed foot surfaces do not stick to the kiln shelf in firing.
Stirring the glaze

Stir the glaze thoroughly before dipping; glaze pigment and frit settle to the bottom on standing. The glaze should be a uniform consistency throughout, with no lumps visible when stirred. Test specific gravity if available, or use the drip test: glaze dripped from a stirrer should flow off in a thin, even stream.
The dip
1. Hold by the foot ring and submerge

Hold the bowl by the waxed foot ring with two or three fingers. Tilt the bowl to one side and lower it into the glaze at an angle so air can escape from inside. Submerge the bowl fully; the exterior must be fully covered.
2. Rotate and lift

Hold submerged for two to three seconds, then rotate the bowl a half-turn as it is lifted out. The rotation ensures even coverage at the lift point where the fingers grip. Tilt the bowl sideways as it clears the glaze surface to allow excess glaze to drain back into the bucket.
3. Check the coverage and touch up
The
glaze dipping

result should be an even, opaque layer of glaze across the entire exterior and over the rim. Finger marks where the bowl was held will show as bare spots; touch these up with a brush loaded with glaze. Any drips on the foot ring area can be cleaned off with a damp sponge.
Interior glazing

For open bowls, glaze the interior separately before or after the exterior dip. Pour about 100 ml of glaze inside the bowl, swirl to coat the entire interior, and drain back into the bucket. Wipe the foot ring area clean after both interior and exterior are done.
What it looks like when it is right

The dried glaze coat is uniform in thickness (typically 1-2 mm) with a slightly chalky or powdery appearance. The foot ring is clean and bare. No drips, runs, or thick build-up at the rim. The glaze layer should feel solid when tapped lightly with a fingernail without powdering off.

items.fix: Add small amounts of water and stir until consistency is correct; submerge for only two to three seconds
items.cause: Glaze too thick (too little water), or submerged too long
items.symptom: Glaze coat too thick; drips and runs when drying

items.fix: Stir glaze fully; wipe bisqueware with a damp sponge; dip in one smooth motion without pausing
items.cause: Glaze not stirred thoroughly before dipping, or bisqueware surface dusty
items.symptom: Glaze coat uneven: thin patches, thick patches

items.fix: Handle bisqueware by the foot ring only; wipe surface before dipping; check glaze consistency
items.cause: Glaze applied too thick, or finger grease on bisqueware surface
